title: Reverse a Linked List
description:
### Objective
Reverse a singly linked list and return the new head. Modify the next pointers in place using an iterative approach. Do not use extra arrays or recursion.

### Function Contract
Implement `__init__(self: any, val: any, next: any)` in Python.
Do not change input/output semantics expected by the tests.

### Input and Output
- Input `list`: type `array` (example: `[1,2,3,4,5]`).
- Return type: `array`.

### Requirements
- Return the exact expected output for every test case.
- Handle edge cases (empty input, duplicates, boundary values) without raising exceptions.
- Target complexity: time `O(n)`, space `O(1)`.

### Constraints
- 0 ≤ n ≤ 10^5 nodes
- Node values fit in 32-bit signed integer
- List may be empty
explanation:
Intuition
At each step, we detach the current node and point it to the growing reversed prefix. Only a constant number of pointers are moved per node, so the method is linear and in place.

Approach
1. Initial Setup: Track two pointers prev and curr.
2. Main Algorithm: Iterate while curr is not None, rewire curr.next to prev and shift both pointers forward.
3. Return Result: Return prev which becomes the new head.

Complexity Analysis
- Time Complexity: O(n) due to a single pass or divide-and-conquer
  over the list where each node is visited a bounded number of times.
- Space Complexity: O(1) accounting for pointers and, when needed,
  recursion depth or extra structures.

Key Insights
- Reversing a list is pointer gymnastics, not value copying
- Thinking in terms of growing a new head clarifies the loop

Common Pitfalls
- Losing reference to the rest of the list without storing next
- Returning curr instead of prev at the end
hints: Keep track of the next node before rewiring
Point current to the previous node
